argumentCard.strongestObjection: The dialogue's relentless criticism of the Forms, combined with the opacity of the dialectical exercise in the second half, may show that Plato recognized fatal problems in his own theory without finding a way to solve them.

title: Theaetetus
author: Plato
introduction: Written around 369 BC, the Theaetetus is Plato's most sustained investigation into the nature of knowledge. Socrates examines three proposed definitions — knowledge is perception, knowledge is true belief, and knowledge is true belief with an account (logos) — and finds all three inadequate. The dialogue anticipates virtually every major problem in modern epistemology, from the challenge of relativism to the Gettier problem, and ends in the rare Socratic position of genuine aporia — acknowledged ignorance.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is knowledge?
argumentCard.thesis: None of the proposed definitions of knowledge — perception, true belief, or true belief with an account — can withstand critical examination; the nature of knowledge remains genuinely puzzling.
keyMoves: Refutes "knowledge is perception" by showing that perception varies between individuals and moments, yet knowledge must be stable and objective — and by dismantling Protagoras' relativism ("man is the measure of all things")
Refutes "knowledge is true belief" by showing that a jury might be persuaded to hold a true belief through rhetoric rather than evidence, yet we would not call this knowledge

Examines "knowledge is true belief with an account (logos)" and shows that every attempt to define "account" either presupposes knowledge (circular) or fails to distinguish knowledge from mere true belief
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The dialogue's entirely negative conclusion — no definition of knowledge succeeds — may reflect not philosophical depth but a genuine inability to solve the problem, leaving the reader no wiser than before.

title: On Duties
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
introduction: Written in 44 BC during the final crisis of the Roman Republic, On Duties is Cicero's practical guide to moral decision-making, addressed to his son Marcus. Drawing on Stoic philosophy while adapting it to Roman political life, Cicero examines the relationship between the honorable (honestum) and the useful (utile), arguing they can never truly conflict. The work became the most widely read pagan moral treatise in the Western world, shaping medieval and Renaissance ethics.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: When moral duty and practical advantage seem to conflict, how should we decide what to do?
argumentCard.thesis: True advantage and true honor can never conflict — what is morally right is always genuinely useful in the long run, and any apparent conflict arises from confusing real advantage with short-term gain.
keyMoves: Identifies four cardinal virtues — wisdom, justice, courage, and temperance — and derives specific duties from each
Argues that justice, especially keeping faith and respecting property, is the foundation of social life and therefore the most important virtue
Demonstrates through historical examples that apparent conflicts between duty and advantage always dissolve when we think clearly about what genuine advantage means
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Cicero's insistence that duty and advantage never truly conflict seems wishful — history provides abundant examples of good people destroyed by doing the right thing, suggesting that virtue sometimes really does come at a cost.

title: On Old Age
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
introduction: Written in 44 BC, On Old Age (De Senectute) is Cicero's consoling dialogue in which the eighty-four-year-old Cato the Elder defends old age against its four chief charges: that it withdraws us from active life, weakens the body, deprives us of pleasures, and brings us closer to death. Blending Stoic philosophy with Roman practical wisdom, the essay argues that a well-lived old age can be the crowning achievement of a good life. It has comforted readers for two millennia.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Is old age a misfortune to be endured, or can it be a fulfilling stage of life?
argumentCard.thesis: Old age, far from being a decline, can be the most rewarding period of life — provided one has cultivated virtue, wisdom, and meaningful activity throughout one's years.
keyMoves: Refutes the charge that old age bars us from active life by showing that wisdom, counsel, and authority — the old person's strengths — are more valuable than youthful energy
Argues that the loss of physical pleasures is actually a liberation, freeing the mind for intellectual and social enjoyment
Addresses the fear of death by arguing that the soul is either immortal (in which case death is a gain) or extinguished (in which case it is nothing to fear)
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Cato's rosy portrait of old age is available only to the wealthy, healthy, and privileged — for most people in the ancient world (and many today), old age meant poverty, pain, and dependence.

title: Tusculan Disputations
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
introduction: Written in 45 BC during Cicero's political exile and personal grief over his daughter's death, the Tusculan Disputations is a series of five philosophical conversations exploring how philosophy can help us face suffering, death, and the turbulence of fortune. Cicero draws on Stoic, Epicurean, and Academic traditions to argue that virtue alone is sufficient for happiness. The work is both a philosophical treatise and a deeply personal document of a mind seeking consolation through reason.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Can philosophy make us resilient against suffering, death, and misfortune?
argumentCard.thesis: The wise person can achieve tranquility and even happiness in the face of any misfortune, because virtue — which depends entirely on our own will — is sufficient for a good life, and the things we typically fear (death, pain, grief) are either not evils at all or can be overcome by philosophical discipline.
keyMoves: Argues that death is not an evil: either the soul survives (and gains a better existence) or it is extinguished (and feels nothing)
Claims that pain can be endured through mental discipline and that the truly wise person is not diminished by physical suffering
Contends that the passions (grief, fear, anger, desire) are not natural and inevitable but arise from false judgments that philosophy can correct
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The claim that virtue alone suffices for happiness asks too much of philosophy — telling a person in extreme physical agony or devastating loss that they can be happy through correct thinking may be not wisdom but callousness.

title: Lives of the Eminent Philosophers
author: Diogenes Laërtius
introduction: Written around the third century AD, Lives of the Eminent Philosophers is our single most important source for the history of ancient Greek philosophy. Diogenes Laërtius compiled biographical sketches, doctrinal summaries, and colorful anecdotes about nearly every major philosopher from Thales to Epicurus. While often uncritical as history, the work preserves fragments, sayings, and details about dozens of thinkers whose own writings have been lost, making it indispensable for understanding the ancient philosophical tradition.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What did the major Greek philosophers teach, and what kind of lives did they lead?
argumentCard.thesis: Philosophy is best understood through the lives and characters of its practitioners — their teachings, their conduct, their witty sayings, and their schools — rather than through abstract doctrinal summaries alone.
keyMoves: Organizes philosophers into two main traditions — the Ionian (from Thales through Socrates to the Stoics) and the Italian (from Pythagoras through Plato to the Academics)
Preserves invaluable summaries of doctrines from lost works, including detailed accounts of Stoic logic, Epicurean physics, and Pyrrhonian skepticism
Emphasizes the unity of philosophical teaching and philosophical living, recording how each thinker's character and conduct embodied (or contradicted) their doctrines
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Diogenes is an uncritical compiler who mixes reliable information with gossip, legend, and fabrication, making it difficult to distinguish historical fact from entertaining fiction.

title: Discourses
author: Epictetus
introduction: Recorded by his student Arrian around 108 AD, the Discourses capture the teaching of Epictetus, a former slave who became one of the most influential Stoic philosophers. In vivid, confrontational lectures, Epictetus hammers home a single transformative insight: some things are in our power (our judgments, desires, and actions) and some are not (our bodies, reputations, and external circumstances), and freedom consists in focusing entirely on the former. The work has inspired thinkers from Marcus Aurelius to modern cognitive behavioral therapy.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: How can we achieve freedom and peace of mind in a world we cannot control?
argumentCard.thesis: True freedom lies in recognizing that only our own judgments, desires, and choices are within our power — everything else (health, wealth, reputation, even life itself) is not ours to control, and suffering comes from confusing the two.
keyMoves: Establishes the fundamental distinction between "things up to us" (prohairetic) and "things not up to us" (aprohairetic), arguing that all philosophy rests on getting this distinction right
Argues that we are disturbed not by events themselves but by our judgments about them — change the judgment and you change the experience
Insists that philosophy is not theoretical knowledge but daily practice — a discipline of assent, desire, and action that must be exercised like a muscle
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The sharp division between what is "up to us" and what is not oversimplifies human experience — our judgments are shaped by our upbringing, biology, and circumstances in ways that make the Stoic's radical inner freedom seem more like an ideal than an achievable reality.

title: Enchiridion
author: Epictetus
introduction: The Enchiridion ("Handbook") is a compact manual of Stoic ethics distilled from Epictetus's longer Discourses by his student Arrian. In just fifty-three short chapters, it delivers the essence of Stoic practical philosophy: distinguish what you can control from what you cannot, accept the latter with equanimity, and focus all your energy on the former. This pocket-sized guide to inner freedom has been carried by soldiers, statesmen, and seekers for nearly two thousand years.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the key to a tranquil and virtuous life?
argumentCard.thesis: The foundation of all philosophy is this: some things are within our control (our opinions, intentions, desires, and aversions) and some are not (our body, property, reputation, and position) — happiness depends entirely on focusing on the former and accepting the latter.
keyMoves: Opens with the fundamental Stoic distinction: "Some things are in our control and others not" — and all misery comes from trying to control what we cannot
Teaches the discipline of desire: want only what is within your power, and you will never be frustrated; fear only what is within your power, and you will never be afraid
Provides practical exercises: when disturbed by an external event, remind yourself it is not the event but your judgment of it that disturbs you
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The Enchiridion's counsel to remain indifferent to everything external — including the suffering and death of loved ones — can seem not like wisdom but like emotional numbness dressed up as philosophy.

title: Meditations
author: Marcus Aurelius
introduction: Written between roughly 170 and 180 AD during military campaigns on the Roman frontier, the Meditations is Marcus Aurelius's private philosophical journal — never intended for publication. The Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher writes to himself about duty, mortality, the insignificance of fame, and the importance of acting justly regardless of circumstances. Intimate, repetitive, and profoundly honest, it is perhaps the most personal document to survive from the ancient world and one of the most beloved works of practical philosophy.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: How should one live and govern justly in a world of constant change and inevitable death?
argumentCard.thesis: Everything external is fleeting and beyond our control — fame, pleasure, power, even life itself — and the only thing that matters is the quality of our inner life: thinking clearly, acting justly, and accepting whatever nature brings with equanimity.
keyMoves: Constantly reminds himself that all things are transient — empires, reputations, even the universe itself — and that clinging to them is the source of suffering
Insists on the duty to act for the common good: as a rational being, you are part of a larger whole, and justice means playing your role well regardless of how others behave
Practices the discipline of perception: strip events of the stories and judgments you attach to them, and see them as they really are — atoms rearranging, nothing more
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Marcus's private struggle to live up to Stoic ideals — his recurring self-reproach and the evident difficulty he has in maintaining equanimity — may reveal that the Stoic program is simply too demanding for human beings, even for a philosopher-emperor.

title: Minor Dialogues
author: Seneca
introduction: Written between roughly 49 and 65 AD, Seneca's Minor Dialogues include some of the finest practical philosophy in the Stoic tradition. Essays like "On the Shortness of Life," "On Anger," and "On the Tranquility of the Mind" address the emotional and practical challenges of daily life with a directness and psychological insight that feel remarkably modern. Seneca writes not as a detached theorist but as a fellow sufferer, struggling to apply Stoic principles to the messy realities of life at Nero's court.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: How can we manage our emotions, use our time wisely, and find peace of mind amid the chaos of life?
argumentCard.thesis: Life is not short — we make it short by wasting time on trivial pursuits, destructive emotions, and anxious preoccupation with the future; by living attentively and according to reason, we can find tranquility and make a brief life feel abundant.
keyMoves: Argues that anger is a form of temporary madness rooted in false judgments and can be prevented by questioning our initial impressions before acting on them
Shows that we squander the majority of our lives on distractions, social obligations, and meaningless busyness while complaining that life is too short
Counsels that tranquility comes not from withdrawing from the world but from engaging with it wisely — choosing our commitments deliberately and accepting reversal with equanimity
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Seneca's immense personal wealth and complicity in Nero's tyranny create a painful gap between his philosophical advice and his actual life — raising the question of whether Stoic wisdom is merely a consolation for the privileged.

title: Leviathan
author: Thomas Hobbes
introduction: Published in 1651 during the English Civil War, Leviathan is Hobbes's masterwork of political philosophy. Starting from a bleak view of human nature — that without government, life would be "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short" — Hobbes constructs a rigorous argument for absolute sovereign authority as the only guarantee of peace and order. The work scandalized contemporaries with its materialism and its reduction of morality to self-interest, but it founded modern political philosophy and social contract theory.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Why do we need government, and what form should it take?
argumentCard.thesis: In the state of nature, rational self-interest leads inevitably to a war of all against all; peace is possible only when individuals surrender their natural rights to an absolute sovereign — the Leviathan — whose power is unlimited and indivisible.
keyMoves: Describes the state of nature as a condition of perpetual war where there is no industry, culture, or society, and every person has a right to everything — including other people's lives
Argues that rational self-interest compels people to agree to a social contract, surrendering their natural freedom to a sovereign in exchange for security
Insists that the sovereign's power must be absolute and undivided — any limitation on sovereign authority creates a space for the return of civil war
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Hobbes's cure may be worse than the disease: an absolute sovereign with unlimited power is just as capable of making life nasty, brutish, and short as the state of nature — tyranny is at least as dangerous as anarchy.

title: On Liberty
author: John Stuart Mill
introduction: Published in 1859, On Liberty is Mill's passionate defense of individual freedom against the "tyranny of the majority." Writing with his wife Harriet Taylor, Mill argues that society may legitimately restrict individual action only to prevent harm to others — never to enforce moral conformity or protect people from themselves. The work is the most influential statement of liberal political philosophy ever written and remains central to debates about free speech, privacy, and the limits of government.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What are the proper limits of society's power over the individual?
argumentCard.thesis: The only legitimate reason for society or government to restrict an individual's freedom is to prevent harm to others — over his own body and mind, the individual is sovereign.
keyMoves: Establishes the "harm principle": power can be rightfully exercised over a member of a civilized community only to prevent harm to others, not to enforce moral or religious conformity
Argues that free speech must be nearly absolute because suppressed opinions might be true, and even false opinions sharpen and vitalize truth through debate
Defends "experiments in living" — individual eccentricity and nonconformity — as essential for social progress and the discovery of better ways of life
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The harm principle is more difficult to apply than Mill suggests — almost any action can be said to "harm" others indirectly, and where we draw the line between harm and mere offense is itself a deeply contested moral question.

title: The Subjection of Women
author: John Stuart Mill
introduction: Published in 1869, The Subjection of Women is Mill's systematic argument that the legal subordination of women to men is wrong in principle and harmful in practice. Drawing on his lifelong intellectual partnership with Harriet Taylor, Mill argues that the customs and laws restricting women are relics of a primitive age of force, not products of reason or nature. The work was far ahead of its time and became a foundational text of the women's rights movement.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Is the legal and social subordination of women justified?
argumentCard.thesis: The subordination of women is not grounded in nature but in custom and force; it is both unjust in principle and harmful in practice, depriving society of the talents and contributions of half the human race.
keyMoves: Argues that we cannot know women's "true nature" because women have always lived under conditions of subordination — what appears natural is merely the result of training and restriction
Shows that the same arguments used to justify women's subordination were once used to justify slavery, and were abandoned not because human nature changed but because moral understanding advanced
Contends that equality between the sexes would double the available pool of talent and intelligence, benefiting not just women but all of society
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Mill's argument rests heavily on utilitarian calculation — equality is good because it produces more happiness and talent — which may leave the case for women's rights vulnerable if someone could show that inequality produces better outcomes.

title: Utilitarianism
author: John Stuart Mill
introduction: Published in 1863, Utilitarianism is Mill's concise defense of the principle that actions are right insofar as they promote the greatest happiness for the greatest number. Mill refines Bentham's earlier formulation by introducing qualitative distinctions among pleasures — insisting that intellectual and moral pleasures are higher than bodily ones — and by arguing that utilitarianism, far from being a cold calculus, captures the deepest moral intuitions of ordinary people. It remains the most widely read statement of utilitarian ethics.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the foundation of morality, and how should we determine right from wrong?
argumentCard.thesis: Actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness (pleasure and the absence of pain) and wrong as they tend to produce the reverse — but not all pleasures are equal; those of the intellect and moral sentiments are qualitatively superior to those of the body.
keyMoves: Defends the Greatest Happiness Principle against the objection that it is a "doctrine worthy of swine" by distinguishing higher pleasures (intellectual, aesthetic, moral) from lower ones (bodily) — "better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied"
Argues that the test of higher pleasure is the judgment of those who have experienced both kinds — competent judges invariably prefer the pleasures of the mind

Shows that utilitarianism does not require calculating consequences for every individual action; we can rely on moral rules ("secondary principles") that have been tested by centuries of human experience
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Mill's distinction between higher and lower pleasures is inconsistent with his own empiricism — who decides which pleasures are "higher," and on what basis, if not by smuggling in a non-utilitarian standard of human excellence?

title: Rights of Man
author: Thomas Paine
introduction: Published in 1791 as a direct reply to Edmund Burke's attack on the French Revolution, Rights of Man is Paine's fiery defense of revolution, democracy, and natural rights. Writing with the directness and moral urgency that had helped spark the American Revolution with Common Sense, Paine argues that every generation has the right to govern itself and that hereditary monarchy and aristocracy are frauds imposed on humanity by force and custom. The work sold hundreds of thousands of copies and nearly got Paine executed for sedition.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Do the people have a right to overthrow their government and establish a new one?
argumentCard.thesis: Every generation has the natural and inalienable right to choose its own form of government — no past generation can bind future ones, and hereditary rule is an absurd violation of human equality and natural rights.
keyMoves: Refutes Burke's argument that the English are bound by the constitutional settlement of 1688, arguing that the dead have no authority over the living
Grounds political legitimacy in natural rights that belong to every individual by birth, not in tradition, custom, or the inherited privileges of aristocracy
Proposes practical social welfare programs — old-age pensions, public education, support for the poor — arguing that just government must actively promote the welfare of its citizens
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Paine's optimistic faith in popular revolution underestimates the dangers of violent upheaval — the French Revolution he championed soon devolved into the Terror, suggesting that Burke's warnings about the costs of radical change were not entirely wrong.

title: Discourse on Inequality
author: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
introduction: Published in 1755, the Discourse on the Origin and Basis of Inequality Among Men is Rousseau's explosive argument that civilization itself is the source of human misery. While Hobbes imagined the state of nature as a war of all against all, Rousseau pictures it as a condition of peaceful independence corrupted by the invention of private property and the social comparisons it enables. The work launched the tradition of critical social theory and remains one of the most radical challenges to the assumption that progress makes us happier.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the origin of inequality among human beings, and is it natural or artificial?
argumentCard.thesis: Natural human beings are solitary, self-sufficient, and compassionate; inequality is not natural but arose from the development of private property, social comparison, and the institutions built to protect the advantages of the powerful.
keyMoves: Imagines a "state of nature" in which humans are peaceful, independent, and guided by self-love (amour de soi) and natural compassion (pitié) — radically unlike Hobbes's warlike vision

Identifies the invention of private property as the decisive turning point: "The first man who, having fenced in a piece of land, said This is mine, and found people naive enough to believe him, was the true founder of civil society"
Traces how social development creates amour-propre (competitive self-love), which replaces natural contentment with envy, vanity, and the desire to dominate others
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Rousseau's idealization of the state of nature relies on speculation rather than evidence, and his account of how society corrupts us offers no practical way back to natural innocence — making it a powerful critique that leads nowhere.

title: The Social Contract
author: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
introduction: Published in 1762 with the famous opening line "Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains," The Social Contract is Rousseau's attempt to solve the fundamental problem of political philosophy: how can people live under government without losing their freedom? His answer — the "general will," a form of collective self-governance where each citizen obeys only laws they have helped to make — became the theoretical inspiration for the French Revolution and for democratic theory ever since.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: How can people live under political authority without sacrificing their freedom?
argumentCard.thesis: Legitimate political authority rests on a social contract in which each citizen surrenders individual rights to the community as a whole, receiving in return an equal share in sovereign power — obeying the "general will" is obeying oneself and therefore remaining free.
keyMoves: Distinguishes the "general will" (the will of the community as a whole, aimed at the common good) from the "will of all" (the mere sum of private interests), arguing that only the general will is truly sovereign
Argues that freedom and authority are reconciled when citizens are both the makers and the subjects of law — political obligation is self-legislation
Insists that sovereignty is inalienable and indivisible — it cannot be delegated to representatives but must be exercised directly by the assembled citizens
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Rousseau's concept of the general will is dangerously ambiguous — if the general will can override individual dissent and "force people to be free," it provides a theoretical foundation for totalitarian democracy.

title: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
author: Mary Wollstonecraft
introduction: Published in 1792 in the ferment of the French Revolution, A Vindication of the Rights of Woman is the founding text of modern feminism. Wollstonecraft argues that women appear inferior to men not because of any natural deficiency but because they are denied education and trained from childhood to be ornamental, dependent, and pleasing. She demands that women receive the same rational education as men, arguing that the health of families, societies, and nations depends on it.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Are women naturally inferior to men, or are they made so by deficient education and social custom?
argumentCard.thesis: Women are rational beings equal to men in their capacity for reason and virtue; their apparent inferiority is entirely the product of an education designed to make them weak, vain, and dependent — and this stunting of half the human race harms everyone.
keyMoves: Argues that Rousseau and other male writers who praise women's "natural" delicacy and emotional sensitivity are really describing the effects of a crippling education, not innate characteristics
Insists that true virtue requires reason, and since women possess reason equally with men, they deserve the same education and the same moral expectations
Contends that the degradation of women degrades men too, because marriages between educated equals are stronger and happier than those between tyrants and slaves
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Wollstonecraft's argument sometimes seems to accept masculine rationality as the standard against which women should be measured, rather than questioning whether the Enlightenment ideal of pure reason is itself adequate to capture the full range of human excellence.

title: Nicomachean Ethics
author: Aristotle
introduction: Composed around 340 BC and named for Aristotle's son Nicomachus, the Nicomachean Ethics is the most influential work of moral philosophy in the Western tradition. Aristotle argues that the goal of human life is eudaimonia — flourishing or happiness — achieved not through pleasure or wealth but through the practice of virtue in accordance with reason. The work's detailed analysis of courage, temperance, justice, friendship, and the contemplative life continues to shape ethical thinking to this day.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the good life for a human being, and how is it achieved?
argumentCard.thesis: The highest human good is eudaimonia (flourishing), which consists in the active exercise of the soul's faculties in accordance with virtue over a complete life — virtue is not a feeling or a natural endowment but a habit developed through practice.
keyMoves: Defines happiness (eudaimonia) as "activity of the soul in accordance with virtue" — not a feeling but a way of living that exercises our distinctly human capacities

Introduces the doctrine of the mean: every moral virtue is a disposition to act at the right point between two extremes (courage between cowardice and recklessness, generosity between stinginess and extravagance)
Argues that we become virtuous by practicing virtuous acts — just as we become builders by building, we become courageous by doing courageous things
Places friendship (philia) at the center of the good life, arguing that the highest form of friendship is based on mutual recognition of virtue
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Aristotle's ethics is shaped by the values of a privileged Greek male — his account of the good life presupposes leisure, wealth, and social status, and he explicitly excludes women, slaves, and manual laborers from full human flourishing.

title: Politics
author: Aristotle
introduction: Composed around 350 BC as a companion to the Nicomachean Ethics, the Politics is Aristotle's investigation into the best form of political community. Arguing that the polis (city-state) exists by nature and that human beings are "political animals," Aristotle examines constitutions, citizenship, education, and the causes of revolution. The work established political science as a systematic discipline and continues to inform debates about democracy, justice, and the purpose of government.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the best form of political community, and how should it be organized?
argumentCard.thesis: The city-state exists by nature because human beings are naturally political animals who can only achieve the good life in community; the best constitution is one that aims at the common good and distributes power according to the citizens' capacity for virtue.
keyMoves: Argues that the state is natural, not conventional — it grows organically from the household to the village to the polis, and only within the polis can humans achieve their full potential
Classifies constitutions into correct forms (monarchy, aristocracy, polity) that aim at the common good and corrupt forms (tyranny, oligarchy, democracy) that serve the rulers' private interests
Advocates a mixed constitution (polity) combining elements of democracy and oligarchy as the most stable and just arrangement for most actual states
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Aristotle's political theory rests on a hierarchical view of human nature that justifies slavery and the exclusion of women from citizenship — undermining his claim to be describing the conditions for universal human flourishing.

title: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
author: Immanuel Kant
introduction: Published in 1785, the Groundwork is Kant's attempt to discover and establish the supreme principle of morality. In clear, rigorous prose, Kant argues that moral worth lies not in the consequences of our actions but in the principle behind them — specifically, in acting from duty according to a law we could will to be universal. The work introduces the categorical imperative, which has become the most famous and debated moral principle in modern philosophy.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the supreme principle of morality?
argumentCard.thesis: The foundation of morality is the categorical imperative: act only according to that maxim which you can at the same time will to be a universal law — and treat humanity, whether in yourself or others, always as an end and never merely as a means.
keyMoves: Argues that the only thing good without qualification is a good will — intelligence, courage, and other talents can be used for evil, but a will that acts from duty is unconditionally good
Derives the categorical imperative as the test of moral duty: before acting, ask whether you could consistently will that everyone act on the same principle
Formulates the "humanity formula": treat rational beings always as ends in themselves, never merely as instruments for your purposes — this grounds the unconditional dignity of persons
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Kant's ethics is too formal and abstract to provide practical guidance — the categorical imperative can seem to generate absurd results (must I tell a murderer where my friend is hiding?) and cannot resolve conflicts between competing duties.

title: Apology
author: Plato
introduction: Written shortly after 399 BC, the Apology is Plato's account of Socrates' defense speech at his trial for impiety and corrupting the youth of Athens. Far from apologizing, Socrates mounts a defiant defense of the philosophical life, arguing that the unexamined life is not worth living and that he would rather die than stop questioning. The work is the most vivid portrait we have of Socrates and the founding document of the Western philosophical tradition.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Is the philosophical life worth living, even at the cost of death?
argumentCard.thesis: The unexamined life is not worth living — the pursuit of wisdom through questioning is the highest human activity, and it is better to die faithful to this calling than to live by abandoning it.
keyMoves: Explains his philosophical mission as obedience to the god at Delphi: told that no one was wiser, Socrates discovered his wisdom consisted only in knowing that he did not know
Argues that his questioning of Athenian leaders, poets, and craftsmen — which earned him enemies — was a service to the city, like a gadfly stinging a lazy horse into action
Refuses to beg for mercy or abandon philosophy to save his life, arguing that fearing death is itself a form of ignorance — pretending to know what we do not
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Socrates' uncompromising stance may reflect intellectual pride rather than genuine wisdom — a wiser person might have found a way to continue philosophizing without needlessly provoking his own execution.

title: Crito
author: Plato
introduction: Set in Socrates' prison cell in 399 BC, the Crito dramatizes a conversation between Socrates and his wealthy friend Crito, who has arranged an escape. Socrates refuses, arguing that it is never right to return injustice for injustice and that he has an obligation to obey the laws of Athens even when they are misapplied. The dialogue raises questions about civil disobedience, the social contract, and the relationship between law and justice that remain urgent today.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Is it ever right to break the law, even when you have been unjustly condemned?
argumentCard.thesis: It is never right to do wrong, even in response to wrong done to you — and since Socrates has benefited from Athens' laws and implicitly agreed to abide by them, he must accept even an unjust verdict rather than undermine the legal order by escaping.
keyMoves: Establishes the principle that it is never right to do wrong or return evil for evil, regardless of what has been done to you

Argues that by living in Athens and benefiting from its laws for seventy years, Socrates has made an implicit agreement to obey them — leaving when the verdict goes against him would be breaking that agreement
Personifies the Laws of Athens, who argue that if citizens disobey whenever they disagree with a verdict, the entire legal order will be destroyed
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Socrates' argument proves too much — if we must always obey the law, there can be no justified civil disobedience, which means figures like Gandhi, Thoreau, and Martin Luther King Jr. were wrong to break unjust laws.

title: Euthyphro
author: Plato
introduction: Set just before Socrates' trial in 399 BC, the Euthyphro finds Socrates questioning a young man who claims to know exactly what piety is. Through relentless questioning, Socrates exposes the inadequacy of every definition Euthyphro offers, culminating in one of philosophy's most famous dilemmas: is something holy because the gods love it, or do the gods love it because it is holy? The dialogue is a masterclass in Socratic method and the starting point for all subsequent debate about the relationship between religion and morality.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is piety, and what is the relationship between the gods and morality?
argumentCard.thesis: None of the proposed definitions of piety can withstand scrutiny, and the most important question — whether things are good because the gods approve them or whether the gods approve them because they are independently good — reveals a fundamental problem in grounding morality in divine authority.
keyMoves: Demolishes Euthyphro's definition that piety is "what the gods love" by noting that the gods disagree, so the same action would be both pious and impious
Poses the Euthyphro dilemma: is something pious because the gods love it (making morality arbitrary) or do the gods love it because it is pious (making morality independent of the gods)?
Shows that Euthyphro's final attempt to define piety as "service to the gods" collapses because he cannot explain what the gods gain from human service
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The dialogue reaches no positive conclusion about what piety is, and the Euthyphro dilemma, while devastating to simple divine command theory, may create a false dichotomy — there might be a third option where God's nature IS the standard of goodness.

title: Gorgias
author: Plato
introduction: Written around 380 BC, the Gorgias is Plato's fiercest dialogue — a dramatic confrontation between Socratic philosophy and the world of rhetoric, power politics, and moral cynicism. Socrates takes on three increasingly aggressive opponents: the rhetorician Gorgias, his student Polus, and the ruthless politician Callicles, who argues that might makes right and that conventional morality is a conspiracy of the weak. The dialogue stakes out Plato's most provocative ethical claims: it is better to suffer injustice than to commit it.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Is it better to be powerful and do whatever you want, or to be just even at the cost of suffering?
argumentCard.thesis: It is better to suffer injustice than to commit it, and better to be punished for wrongdoing than to escape punishment — because injustice corrupts the soul, which is a far greater harm than any bodily suffering.
keyMoves: Argues that rhetoric, as practiced by the sophists, is not a genuine art but a form of flattery — it aims at pleasure rather than truth and can make the worse cause appear the better
Refutes Polus by arguing that tyrants, despite their apparent power, are actually the most pitiable people because doing wrong without punishment leaves the soul diseased
Confronts Callicles' argument that the strong should dominate the weak by showing that even "natural justice" requires some standard of what is truly good, which cannot be reduced to mere power
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Socrates' claim that it is better to suffer injustice than to commit it, while noble in theory, seems to demand an almost inhuman level of detachment from bodily well-being — most people, honestly reflecting, would not choose to be tortured rather than tell a lie.

title: Protagoras
author: Plato
introduction: Written around 380 BC, the Protagoras dramatizes a spirited encounter between Socrates and the great sophist Protagoras over whether virtue can be taught. In one of Plato's most entertaining dialogues, Protagoras tells the myth of Prometheus and Epimetheus to argue that all citizens share political virtue, while Socrates presses his paradoxical view that virtue is knowledge and no one does wrong willingly. The dialogue ends with a delightful reversal: each debater has switched to the other's original position.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Can virtue be taught?
argumentCard.thesis: Virtue is a form of knowledge — specifically, the art of measuring pleasures and pains to achieve the best overall life — and since knowledge can be taught, virtue can be taught; moreover, no one who truly knows the good would choose evil.
keyMoves: Challenges Protagoras to explain how virtue can be taught when the most virtuous Athenians seem unable to transmit their virtue to their children
Develops the thesis that all virtues (courage, temperance, justice, wisdom) are really one — knowledge of the good — and that apparent cases of weakness of will are actually cases of ignorance
Argues that what people call "being overcome by pleasure" is really a failure of calculation — a mistake about which course of action will produce the greatest overall good
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Socrates' identification of virtue with knowledge seems to deny the reality of weakness of will (akrasia), which is one of the most common and well-documented features of human moral experience — people routinely do what they know to be wrong.

title: Republic
author: Plato
introduction: Composed around 375 BC, the Republic is Plato's masterwork and one of the most influential books ever written. Beginning with the question "What is justice?" Socrates constructs an ideal city-state in speech, arguing that justice in the state mirrors justice in the soul. The dialogue introduces the Theory of Forms, the Allegory of the Cave, the philosopher-king, and a radical vision of communal living that has provoked readers for over two thousand years.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is justice, and is the just person happier than the unjust?
argumentCard.thesis: Justice is the proper ordering of the soul's three parts — reason, spirit, and appetite — with reason ruling; the just person is always happier than the unjust, because a well-ordered soul functions harmoniously while an unjust soul is at war with itself.
keyMoves: Constructs an ideal city with three classes — rulers (philosopher-kings), guardians (warriors), and producers — arguing that justice in the state consists in each class performing its proper function

Draws a parallel between the state and the soul: just as the ideal city has three classes, the soul has three parts (reason, spirit, appetite), and individual justice consists in each part doing its job under reason's rule
Introduces the Allegory of the Cave to illustrate the philosopher's journey from the shadows of opinion to the sunlight of true knowledge — the Form of the Good
Argues that only philosophers, who have seen the Form of the Good, are qualified to rule, because only they understand what is truly beneficial for the whole community
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Plato's ideal state is authoritarian — it requires censorship, the abolition of private property and family for the ruling class, and rule by an unelected elite, making it a blueprint for totalitarianism rather than justice.

title: The Theory of Moral Sentiments
author: Adam Smith
introduction: Published in 1759, seventeen years before The Wealth of Nations, The Theory of Moral Sentiments is Adam Smith's first and in many ways more fundamental work. Smith argues that morality is grounded not in reason or divine command but in sympathy — our natural ability to imaginatively enter into the feelings of others. By introducing the "impartial spectator" as the internalized judge of our conduct, Smith offers a sophisticated account of moral judgment that anticipates modern psychology and remains a powerful alternative to both utilitarian and Kantian ethics.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the foundation of moral judgment, and how do we distinguish right from wrong?
argumentCard.thesis: Moral judgment is grounded in sympathy — our ability to imaginatively share the feelings of others — and we evaluate our own conduct by asking whether an impartial spectator, fully informed of our situation, would approve of our actions and sentiments.
keyMoves: Identifies sympathy (fellow-feeling) as the foundation of all moral judgment: we approve of others' feelings and actions when we can enter into them imaginatively, and disapprove when we cannot
Introduces the "impartial spectator" — an internalized, well-informed observer whose imagined approval or disapproval guides our moral self-assessment
Argues that the virtues of self-command, prudence, justice, and benevolence arise naturally from the process of sympathetic moral judgment, without needing to be imposed by external authority
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Smith's reliance on an "impartial spectator" may simply project the moral prejudices of his own society — what seems natural and obvious to a well-informed observer in eighteenth-century Scotland might be mere cultural convention rather than genuine moral truth.

title: The Essence of Christianity
author: Ludwig Feuerbach
introduction: Published in 1841, The Essence of Christianity argues that the attributes humans ascribe to God are actually projections of idealized human qualities. Feuerbach contends that theology is really anthropology in disguise, and that recovering this insight frees humanity to redirect its devotion toward the species itself. The work profoundly influenced Marx and Engels and helped launch the materialist turn in German philosophy.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the true content of religious belief?
argumentCard.thesis: Religion is the unconscious self-projection of human nature: God is humanity's own essence externalized and worshipped as an alien being.
keyMoves: Shows each divine attribute (love, reason, will) mirrors an idealized human capacity
Distinguishes the "true" religion of feeling from the false theology of dogma
Argues that recognizing projection liberates humans to value their own species-being
argumentCard.strongestObjection: If religion is mere projection, why have so many cultures independently generated strikingly different conceptions of the divine rather than a single mirror image of humanity?

title: Science of Logic
author: G.W.F. Hegel
introduction: First published between 1812 and 1816, the Science of Logic is Hegel's attempt to derive the fundamental categories of thought through their own internal contradictions. Beginning with the bare concept of "Being," Hegel shows how each category passes into its opposite and is preserved in a higher synthesis. The work establishes the dialectical method that would shape philosophy, theology, and social theory for the next two centuries.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Can the categories of pure thought be systematically derived from one another?
argumentCard.thesis: Beginning from the emptiest concept (Being), every logical category generates its own negation and is sublated into a richer category, culminating in the Absolute Idea.
keyMoves: Demonstrates that pure Being, having no determination, is indistinguishable from Nothing
Introduces Becoming as the unity that sublates Being and Nothing
Unfolds the entire system through successive triadic developments of quality, quantity, essence, and concept
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The transitions often appear to smuggle in content that is not strictly generated by the preceding category, raising the suspicion that the dialectic is guided by Hegel's desired conclusions rather than logical necessity.

title: Discourses on Livy
author: Niccolo Machiavelli
introduction: Composed around 1517 and published posthumously in 1531, the Discourses on Livy examines the first ten books of Livy's history of Rome to extract lessons about republican government. Machiavelli argues that well-ordered republics are more durable than principalities because internal conflict between classes, properly channeled, strengthens rather than weakens the state. The work is the fullest statement of Machiavelli's republican political theory.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What makes a republic endure and flourish?
argumentCard.thesis: A republic achieves greatness when its institutions channel the inevitable conflict between the people and the nobles into laws that serve the common good.
keyMoves: Uses Roman history as empirical evidence for general political principles
Argues that class conflict (tumults) produced Rome's best laws rather than weakening it
Insists that periodic renewal — returning to founding principles — prevents institutional corruption
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Rome's republic ultimately collapsed into civil war and dictatorship, suggesting that the very conflicts Machiavelli praises may be self-destructive in the long run.

title: The Prince
author: Niccolo Machiavelli
introduction: Written in 1513 and published posthumously in 1532, The Prince is a treatise on political power that broke sharply with the moralized advice-to-rulers genre. Machiavelli argues that a prince must be willing to act against conventional morality when necessity demands, because politics operates by its own logic. The work scandalized its contemporaries and remains a foundational text of modern political realism.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: How should a new prince acquire and maintain political power?
argumentCard.thesis: A successful prince must learn how not to be good, adapting his conduct to fortune and necessity rather than adhering to fixed moral ideals.
keyMoves: Separates political effectiveness from Christian virtue, focusing on what rulers actually do rather than what they should do
Argues it is safer to be feared than loved, though a prince should avoid being hated
Introduces fortuna as a force that can be mastered by decisive action (virtu)
argumentCard.strongestObjection: By divorcing politics from ethics, Machiavelli provides a handbook for tyranny and offers no principled limit on the cruelties a ruler may inflict in the name of necessity.

title: The Communist Manifesto
author: Karl Marx
introduction: Published in 1848 on the eve of revolutions across Europe, the Communist Manifesto presents history as a series of class struggles culminating in the conflict between bourgeoisie and proletariat. Marx and Engels argue that capitalism, for all its productive power, generates the very class that will overthrow it. The pamphlet became the most widely read political document of the modern era.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the historical trajectory of class society, and what comes next?
argumentCard.thesis: All history is the history of class struggles; capitalism creates the proletariat, which will abolish private property and class distinctions altogether.
keyMoves: Traces how the bourgeoisie revolutionized production while simultaneously creating its own gravedigger
Argues that the state is merely the executive committee of the ruling class
Calls for the abolition of private property as the precondition for universal human emancipation
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The predicted immiseration and revolutionary consciousness of the proletariat did not materialize in the most advanced capitalist nations, suggesting the historical mechanism is far less deterministic than Marx claimed.

title: Sophist
author: Plato
introduction: Composed around 360 BCE, the Sophist tackles the problem of falsehood and non-being that Parmenides had declared impossible. Through a visiting Eleatic Stranger, Plato revises his own theory of Forms by showing that the great kinds (Being, Sameness, Difference, Motion, Rest) blend with one another. The dialogue is a landmark in the history of logic and ontology.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: How is false statement possible if non-being cannot be spoken of?
argumentCard.thesis: Non-being is not absolute nothingness but Difference — to say what is not is to say what is other than what is — and this makes falsehood intelligible.
keyMoves: Uses the method of division (diairesis) to systematically classify the sophist
Introduces the five "greatest kinds" and shows how they combine and exclude one another
Commits "parricide" against Parmenides by demonstrating that non-being must in some sense be
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The resolution redefines non-being so radically (as mere difference) that it may not address the original Parmenidean challenge about absolute non-existence.

title: What Is Property?
author: Pierre-Joseph Proudhon
introduction: Published in 1840, What Is Property? electrified European radicals with its provocative answer: "Property is theft." Proudhon distinguishes between possession (legitimate use) and property (the right to extract income from others' labor without working). Drawing on natural-law reasoning and economic analysis, he argues that property violates the principles of equality and justice. The work is foundational to the anarchist tradition.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Is private property a natural right?
argumentCard.thesis: Property — defined as the right to profit from another's labor — is theft, because it violates the equality that justice requires.
keyMoves: Distinguishes legitimate possession (personal use) from exploitative property (rent, profit, interest)
Shows that collective labor produces more than the sum of individual efforts, and the surplus is unjustly appropriated by owners
Argues that neither occupation, labor, nor legal convention can justify the institution of property
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Proudhon's distinction between possession and property is unstable: any productive use of land or tools eventually generates the surpluses he wants to prohibit, making his line between legitimate and illegitimate holding unclear.

title: Categories
author: Aristotle
introduction: Written around 350 BCE, the Categories is the opening treatise of Aristotle's logical works (the Organon) and classifies the most basic kinds of predication. Aristotle identifies ten categories — substance, quantity, quality, relation, and six others — under which everything that can be said of a subject falls. The work laid the groundwork for formal logic and ontology for over two millennia.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What are the most fundamental types of being that can be predicated of a subject?
argumentCard.thesis: Everything that is said without combination falls into one of ten categories, with substance (the individual thing) being primary and the others depending on it.
keyMoves: Distinguishes primary substance (individual things) from secondary substance (species and genera)
Enumerates ten categories as the ultimate classifications of predication
Shows that all non-substance categories inhere in or are said of substances
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The list of ten categories appears somewhat arbitrary; Aristotle provides no systematic derivation, and later philosophers have questioned whether the list is complete or whether some categories are reducible to others.

title: Creative Intelligence
author: John Dewey
introduction: Published in 1917, Creative Intelligence collects essays advancing the pragmatist view that thinking is not a passive mirror of reality but an active instrument for solving problems. Dewey argues that intelligence is creative precisely because it reconstructs situations rather than merely copying antecedent facts. The work articulates the instrumentalist logic that Dewey would develop throughout his career.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the nature and function of intelligence?
argumentCard.thesis: Intelligence is not contemplation of fixed truths but the creative reorganization of experience to resolve genuinely problematic situations.
keyMoves: Rejects the spectator theory of knowledge in favor of an active, experimental model
Argues that ideas are plans of action whose meaning lies in their consequences
Shows that inquiry transforms both the knower and the situation known
argumentCard.strongestObjection: If intelligence is defined entirely by practical problem-solving, it may have difficulty accounting for purely theoretical inquiry or aesthetic contemplation that has no immediate practical aim.

title: The Principles of Science
author: William Stanley Jevons
introduction: Published in 1874, The Principles of Science is a comprehensive treatise on scientific method that synthesizes formal logic, probability theory, and the philosophy of induction. Jevons argues that all reasoning, including scientific inference, is fundamentally deductive and that induction is really inverse deduction — reasoning from consequences back to causes. The work anticipates key ideas in the philosophy of science.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What logical principles underlie scientific reasoning?
argumentCard.thesis: All valid inference, including induction, rests on deductive logic; what we call induction is the inverse application of deduction, moving from observed effects to probable causes via the theory of probability.
keyMoves: Reduces induction to inverse deduction using probability calculus
Develops a substitutional theory of logic where reasoning consists in substituting equals for equals
Argues that scientific laws are always probabilistic and subject to revision by new evidence
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Treating induction as inverse deduction may simply relocate the problem: assigning prior probabilities to hypotheses requires assumptions that cannot themselves be grounded in pure deduction.

title: New Essays on Human Understanding
author: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz
introduction: Written in 1704 but published posthumously in 1765, the New Essays is Leibniz's chapter-by-chapter response to Locke's Essay Concerning Human Understanding. Cast as a dialogue between Philalethes (representing Locke) and Theophilus (Leibniz), the work defends innate ideas, the distinction between necessary and contingent truths, and a rationalist theory of knowledge. It is the fullest statement of Leibniz's epistemology.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Are all ideas derived from sense experience, as Locke claims?
argumentCard.thesis: The mind is not a blank slate but contains innate ideas and principles as virtual dispositions that experience occasions but does not create.
keyMoves: Modifies the innate-ideas doctrine: ideas are not consciously present at birth but are dispositional, like veins in marble
Distinguishes truths of reason (necessary, based on the principle of contradiction) from truths of fact (contingent, based on sufficient reason)
Introduces petites perceptions — unconscious micro-perceptions — to explain how experience activates innate structures
argumentCard.strongestObjection: If innate ideas are merely "dispositions" requiring experience to become conscious, the distinction from Locke's empiricism becomes so thin that it risks being a verbal rather than substantive disagreement.

title: Mysticism and Logic
author: Bertrand Russell
introduction: Published in 1918, Mysticism and Logic collects essays in which Russell argues that philosophy must be scientific in method while remaining ambitious in scope. He contrasts the mystical impulse (unity, timelessness, higher reality) with the logical impulse (analysis, precision, respect for facts), arguing that the best philosophy combines the grandeur of the first with the discipline of the second. Several essays introduce key ideas in logic, mathematics, and epistemology.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the proper method and temperament for philosophy?
argumentCard.thesis: Philosophy achieves its best results when it unites the breadth and ambition of mysticism with the precision and intellectual honesty of scientific logic.
keyMoves: Identifies four marks of mysticism (intuition over reason, unity, denial of time, evil as appearance) and subjects each to logical scrutiny
Defends the reality of relations and plurality against monistic idealism
Argues that mathematical logic provides philosophy with a genuinely rigorous method for the first time
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Russell's sharp dichotomy between mysticism and logic may be too neat: many productive philosophical positions (phenomenology, pragmatism) fit comfortably into neither camp.

title: The Art of Controversy
author: Arthur Schopenhauer
introduction: Composed around 1831 and published posthumously, The Art of Controversy (also known as Eristic Dialectic) catalogues thirty-eight stratagems for winning arguments regardless of the truth. Schopenhauer treats debate as a practical skill distinct from logic, observing that human vanity makes people defend positions long after they have been refuted. The work is a uniquely cynical contribution to informal logic and rhetoric.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: How do people actually win arguments, as opposed to how they should reason?
argumentCard.thesis: Dialectical combat follows its own strategic logic: a set of recurring stratagems can be identified and deployed to prevail in debate independently of the truth of one's position.
keyMoves: Distinguishes eristic dialectic (winning) from logic (truth-seeking)
Catalogues 38 stratagems including extension, homonymy, ad hominem, and the appeal to authority
Argues that dishonest tactics are universal because human vanity prevents honest concession
argumentCard.strongestObjection: By treating debate purely as strategic combat, Schopenhauer risks endorsing intellectual bad faith and undermining the cooperative norms that make reasoned discourse possible.

title: On the Fourfold Root of the Principle of Sufficient Reason
author: Arthur Schopenhauer
introduction: First published as Schopenhauer's doctoral dissertation in 1813 and revised in 1847, this work argues that the principle of sufficient reason — nothing is without a reason — takes four distinct forms depending on the class of objects involved. Schopenhauer identifies roots in causality (physical), logic (formal), mathematics (spatial/temporal), and motivation (volitional). The treatise provides the epistemological foundation for The World as Will and Idea.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: How many fundamentally different forms does the principle of sufficient reason take?
argumentCard.thesis: The principle of sufficient reason is not one principle but four, each governing a distinct class of objects for the knowing subject: physical causation, logical ground, mathematical necessity, and motivation.
keyMoves: Distinguishes four classes of objects (representations) and assigns each its own form of the principle
Shows that confusing these four roots produces philosophical errors (e.g., applying causality to things-in-themselves)
Uses the fourfold distinction to critique previous philosophers, especially the ontological argument
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The fourfold classification may be somewhat arbitrary; Schopenhauer never fully explains why there must be exactly four roots rather than more or fewer.

title: Summa Theologica
author: Thomas Aquinas
introduction: Composed between 1265 and 1274, the Summa Theologica is Aquinas's systematic synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy and Christian theology. Organized into questions and articles using the disputatio method, the work covers the existence and nature of God, human nature and ethics, and the person and work of Christ. It remains the most influential work of scholastic philosophy.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Can the truths of faith and the truths of reason be harmonized into a single systematic account?
argumentCard.thesis: Faith and reason are complementary: philosophy can demonstrate certain theological truths (God's existence, natural law) while revelation supplies truths (Trinity, Incarnation) that exceed reason but do not contradict it.
keyMoves: Presents five ways (quinque viae) to demonstrate God's existence from motion, causation, contingency, degrees of perfection, and teleology
Develops natural law theory grounding ethics in human nature's rational participation in eternal law
Uses the Aristotelian framework of act/potency, form/matter, and the four causes throughout
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The harmonization of Aristotle and Christian doctrine may distort both: Aristotle's unmoved mover is not the personal God of Christianity, and forcing them together may compromise philosophical rigor.

title: Philosophy and Theology
author: Averroes
introduction: Written around 1180, this treatise (also known as the Decisive Treatise) argues that philosophy and Islamic law are not only compatible but that the Quran itself commands the study of philosophy for those intellectually capable. Averroes distinguishes three classes of people who interpret scripture differently and argues that apparent conflicts between philosophy and revelation arise from misinterpretation. The work profoundly influenced both Islamic and Latin scholastic thought.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Is the study of philosophy permissible or even required by Islamic law?
argumentCard.thesis: Philosophy (demonstrative reasoning) is obligatory for those capable of it, and where its conclusions appear to conflict with scripture, the scriptural text should be interpreted allegorically.
keyMoves: Argues from Quranic verses that God commands rational investigation of the created world
Distinguishes rhetorical, dialectical, and demonstrative classes of interpretation suited to different intellectual capacities
Contends that only demonstrative (philosophical) interpretation reaches certain truth, while the masses properly rely on literal readings
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Granting philosophers the authority to allegorize scripture whenever it conflicts with their conclusions effectively subordinates revelation to reason, undermining the independent authority of religious texts.

title: The Consolation of Philosophy
author: Boethius
introduction: Written around 524 CE while Boethius awaited execution for treason, the Consolation of Philosophy is a dialogue between the imprisoned author and Lady Philosophy. Alternating between prose and verse, the work argues that true happiness lies not in fortune's gifts but in the contemplation of the highest good. It was one of the most widely read books in medieval Europe and profoundly shaped the Christian intellectual tradition.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: How can a good person find happiness in a world ruled by apparent injustice and blind fortune?
argumentCard.thesis: Fortune's gifts (wealth, power, fame) are inherently unstable and cannot provide true happiness; only the supreme good — God, understood through reason — offers lasting fulfillment.
keyMoves: Personifies Philosophy as a healer who diagnoses the prisoner's spiritual illness as attachment to fortune
Argues that evil has no real existence — it is a privation of good — and that wicked people harm themselves most
Reconciles divine providence with human free will by distinguishing God's eternal perspective from temporal sequence
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The consolation relies on an otherworldly conception of happiness that may seem cold comfort to the actually suffering: telling the condemned that true goods are invisible does not address the reality of unjust punishment.

title: Academica
author: Cicero
introduction: Written in 45 BCE, the Academica presents the epistemological debate between the Academic Skeptics and the Stoics through Roman interlocutors. Cicero defends the New Academy's position that certainty is unattainable but that we can act on probable impressions. The work is our most detailed source for ancient Academic skepticism and its disputes with dogmatic philosophy.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Can we achieve certain knowledge, or must we settle for probability?
argumentCard.thesis: Certainty is unattainable because no impression carries an intrinsic mark distinguishing it from a false one; we should suspend judgment and follow the probable.
keyMoves: Presents the Stoic theory of kataleptic (graspable) impressions, then shows that true and false impressions can be indistinguishable
Defends the Academic method of arguing on both sides of every question to approximate truth
Distinguishes practical assent (following the probable) from theoretical certainty (which is denied)
argumentCard.strongestObjection: If no impression is reliably true, the Academic's own arguments undermine themselves: the claim that certainty is impossible would itself be uncertain, making the position self-refuting.

title: Philosophy of Mind
author: G.W.F. Hegel
introduction: First published as the third part of the Encyclopaedia of the Philosophical Sciences in 1817 and revised through 1830, the Philosophy of Mind traces the development of spirit (Geist) from subjective consciousness through social institutions to absolute knowledge. Hegel argues that mind is not a static substance but a self-developing activity that realizes freedom through progressively more adequate forms. The work connects his logic to his political and cultural philosophy.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is mind, and how does it achieve self-knowledge and freedom?
argumentCard.thesis: Mind (Geist) is essentially self-determining activity that develops through subjective, objective, and absolute stages, achieving full freedom only when it comprehends its own rational nature.
keyMoves: Traces subjective mind from sensation and feeling through representation to rational thought
Shows objective mind realizing itself in law, morality, and ethical life (Sittlichkeit)
Culminates in absolute mind: art, religion, and philosophy as spirit's complete self-comprehension
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The claim that history and culture are the self-development of a single rational spirit imposes a teleological narrative that may distort the contingency and plurality of actual human experience.

title: The Guide for the Perplexed
author: Moses Maimonides
introduction: Completed around 1190, The Guide for the Perplexed addresses educated Jews torn between Aristotelian philosophy and biblical faith. Maimonides argues that scriptural anthropomorphisms must be read allegorically and that the philosophical proofs of God's existence, unity, and incorporeality are compatible with Torah. The work became the most important medieval Jewish philosophical text and deeply influenced Christian and Islamic thought.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: How should a philosophically trained believer interpret apparent conflicts between reason and scripture?
argumentCard.thesis: Reason and revelation are harmonious when scripture is properly interpreted: anthropomorphic language about God is metaphorical, and philosophy demonstrates truths that the Torah presupposes.
keyMoves: Systematically reinterprets every biblical anthropomorphism as metaphor to preserve divine incorporeality
Uses Aristotelian proofs to establish God's existence, unity, and radical otherness (negative theology)
Argues that creation ex nihilo, though not demonstrable, is more rationally defensible than Aristotle's eternal universe
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Maimonides's negative theology — we can only say what God is not — threatens to empty the concept of God of all content, making it unclear what believers are actually affirming.

title: Utopia
author: Thomas More
introduction: Published in 1516, Utopia describes an imaginary island commonwealth where private property has been abolished and citizens live in rational harmony. More uses the traveler Raphael Hythloday's account of Utopian society to critique the injustices of contemporary Europe, especially enclosure, poverty, and the corruption of rulers. The work invented the literary genre of utopian fiction and remains a touchstone for political philosophy.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What would a truly just society look like, and what prevents its realization?
argumentCard.thesis: A just society requires the abolition of private property, because as long as wealth is unequally distributed, the common good will always be sacrificed to private interest.
keyMoves: Contrasts European corruption (poverty amid wealth, unjust punishment, warmongering) with Utopian rationality
Describes Utopian institutions — communal property, six-hour workdays, religious tolerance — as products of reason alone
Uses the dialogue frame to maintain ironic distance, leaving it ambiguous how much More himself endorses
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The Utopian system depends on a degree of regimentation and conformity (assigned housing, travel permits, uniform dress) that amounts to an oppressive denial of individual freedom.

title: The Logic of Chance
author: John Venn
introduction: Published in 1866, The Logic of Chance develops the frequency interpretation of probability, arguing that probability statements are about the long-run behavior of series of events rather than degrees of belief. Venn contends that probability is an objective, empirical matter and criticizes subjective and a priori approaches. The work laid important groundwork for modern frequentist statistics.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What do probability statements actually mean?
argumentCard.thesis: Probability is the limiting frequency of an attribute in a series of events; it is an objective, empirical property of the world, not a measure of subjective ignorance or rational belief.
keyMoves: Defines probability as the ratio of favorable outcomes in an indefinitely extended series of similar events
Criticizes the classical (Laplacean) approach of counting equally possible cases as circular
Argues that probability applies to series, not to individual events, resolving apparent paradoxes
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The frequency interpretation cannot assign probabilities to unique, non-repeatable events (a particular election, a specific scientific hypothesis), which limits its applicability to many real-world problems.

title: Notes from Underground
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
introduction: Published in 1864, Notes from Underground is a novella-length monologue by a bitter, self-aware retired civil servant who rebels against the rational determinism of his age. The Underground Man insists that human beings will act against their own interests simply to assert their freedom, exposing the limits of utilitarian and materialist philosophies. The work is widely regarded as the first existentialist novel.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Can human behavior be reduced to rational self-interest?
argumentCard.thesis: Human beings will deliberately choose suffering and irrationality over a rationally ordered paradise, because free will — even self-destructive free will — is more fundamental than happiness.
keyMoves: Attacks the "crystal palace" of rational utopia as a denial of individual caprice and spontaneity
Argues that consciousness itself is a disease: the more aware one becomes, the less capable of action
Shows through the narrator's own behavior that spite, vanity, and perversity resist systematic explanation
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The Underground Man's rebellion against reason is itself a kind of reasoning, and his self-awareness about his contradictions suggests that rationality cannot be escaped as easily as he claims.

title: Selections from the Writings of Kierkegaard
author: Soren Kierkegaard
introduction: This collection draws from Kierkegaard's major works of the 1840s, including Either/Or, Fear and Trembling, and The Sickness Unto Death. Kierkegaard argues that existence cannot be captured by abstract systems and that the individual must make passionate, subjective choices — especially the "leap of faith" — that no rational philosophy can justify. His writings founded the existentialist tradition in philosophy.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What does it mean to exist as a concrete individual rather than an abstract thinker?
argumentCard.thesis: Authentic existence requires passionate subjective commitment through stages (aesthetic, ethical, religious) that cannot be mediated by rational systems — truth is subjectivity.
keyMoves: Distinguishes three existence-spheres and shows that movement between them requires a qualitative leap, not logical progression
Uses pseudonyms to enact different existential standpoints from within rather than describing them from outside
Argues that the highest form of existence (religious) demands a "leap of faith" that contradicts universal ethical norms, as in Abraham's sacrifice of Isaac
argumentCard.strongestObjection: If truth is purely subjective and rational justification is abandoned, there is no way to distinguish authentic faith from fanaticism or self-delusion.

title: Beyond Good and Evil
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
introduction: Published in 1886, Beyond Good and Evil critiques the dogmatic tradition in philosophy and proposes a radical revaluation of moral values. Nietzsche distinguishes "master morality" (which affirms strength and nobility) from "slave morality" (which valorizes meekness and resentment), arguing that modern European morality is a disguised expression of the latter. The work prepares the ground for the Genealogy of Morals.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What lies beneath the surface of our moral convictions?
argumentCard.thesis: Every philosophy is an unconscious confession of its author's drives; modern morality, rooted in slave resentment, must be overcome by "free spirits" who create values beyond the good/evil dichotomy.
keyMoves: Exposes the will to truth as itself a form of will to power that philosophers refuse to examine
Distinguishes master morality (good/bad) from slave morality (good/evil) as competing valuations
Calls for "philosophers of the future" who legislate new values rather than merely discovering pre-existing ones
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Nietzsche's critique of morality as disguised power seems to apply reflexively to his own philosophy: if all value-claims express will to power, his own revaluation has no privileged status.
