title: The Birth of Tragedy
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
introduction: Published in 1872, The Birth of Tragedy argues that Greek tragedy arose from the creative tension between two aesthetic impulses: the Apollonian (form, order, individuation) and the Dionysian (intoxication, dissolution, primal unity). Nietzsche contends that Socratic rationalism destroyed tragedy by demanding that art justify itself before the tribunal of reason, and he looks to Wagner's music-drama for its rebirth. The work launched Nietzsche's philosophical career.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What gave rise to Greek tragedy, and why did it die?
argumentCard.thesis: Tragedy was born from the fusion of Apollonian form and Dionysian ecstasy; it died when Socratic optimism — the belief that reason can master existence — replaced tragic wisdom with dialectical inquiry.
keyMoves: Identifies two fundamental aesthetic drives (Apollonian and Dionysian) rooted in dream and intoxication
Argues that tragedy made life bearable by giving beautiful form to the terrifying Dionysian truth of existence
Blames Euripides and Socrates for killing tragedy by demanding rational intelligibility and moral lessons from art
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The historical narrative is tendentious: the decline of tragedy had multiple causes (political, institutional, aesthetic), and blaming Socrates conflates dramatic history with Nietzsche's own anti-rationalist agenda.

title: On the Genealogy of Morals
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
introduction: Published in 1887, the Genealogy of Morals traces the historical origins of moral concepts through three essays on "good and evil," "guilt and bad conscience," and the meaning of ascetic ideals. Nietzsche argues that morality is not timeless but emerged from specific power relations — particularly the "slave revolt" in which the weak revalued strength as evil. The work pioneered the genealogical method later adopted by Foucault and others.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Where do our moral concepts actually come from?
argumentCard.thesis: The concepts of "good" and "evil" originated in a slave revolt in morality, where the powerless revalued the qualities of the strong as "evil" and their own weakness as "good" — a transformation driven by resentment (ressentiment).
keyMoves: Distinguishes the noble valuation (good = strong, beautiful) from the priestly revaluation (good = meek, suffering)
Traces guilt and bad conscience to the internalization of aggressive instincts when civilization suppressed their outward expression
Exposes ascetic ideals as the will to nothingness — the last disguise of the will to power in those who cannot affirm life
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The genealogical method cannot settle normative questions: even if morality originated in resentment, it does not follow that its current content is invalid or that we should abandon it.

title: Thus Spake Zarathustra
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
introduction: Published in parts between 1883 and 1885, Thus Spake Zarathustra is Nietzsche's philosophical novel in which the prophet Zarathustra descends from his mountain to teach humanity about the Ubermensch (overman) and the eternal recurrence. Written in a biblical-poetic style, the work proclaims the death of God and challenges humanity to create new values worthy of the earth. It is Nietzsche's most ambitious and literary philosophical work.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What should humanity aspire to after the death of God?
argumentCard.thesis: Humanity is a bridge to the Ubermensch — a being who creates its own values, affirms life completely (eternal recurrence), and overcomes the nihilism left by the death of God.
keyMoves: Announces the death of God and diagnoses the resulting crisis of meaning
Introduces the Ubermensch as the goal of human self-overcoming, replacing divine purpose with self-created values
Presents the eternal recurrence as the supreme test of life-affirmation: could you will this exact life again infinitely?
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The Ubermensch ideal is dangerously vague: without substantive content, it can be appropriated to justify any project of domination, as its historical misuse demonstrates.

title: Pensees
author: Blaise Pascal
introduction: Published posthumously in 1670, the Pensees are fragments of an unfinished apology for the Christian religion. Pascal argues that human reason is too limited to resolve the question of God's existence, but that the heart has reasons that reason cannot know. His famous "wager" reframes belief as a practical decision under uncertainty. The work combines mathematical rigor with existential intensity.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Can reason alone lead us to God, or must we look beyond it?
argumentCard.thesis: Reason reveals its own limits; the human condition — caught between infinity and nothingness — demands a leap beyond rational demonstration, making faith a rational wager and an affair of the heart.
keyMoves: Diagnoses the human condition as wretched without God: boredom, diversion, and self-deception mask our misery
Presents the wager: belief in God is the rational bet because the potential gain (eternal life) infinitely outweighs the finite cost
Argues that the heart has its own order of knowledge — intuition of first principles — that precedes and grounds discursive reason
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The wager reduces faith to a calculated gamble and can apply equally to any religion; it cannot tell us which God to believe in or whether genuine faith can be produced by prudential reasoning.

title: The World as Will and Idea
author: Arthur Schopenhauer
introduction: Published in 1818, The World as Will and Idea argues that the world has a dual nature: as representation (Idea), it is governed by space, time, and causality; as thing-in-itself, it is blind, striving Will. Schopenhauer contends that this restless Will is the source of all suffering, and that release comes only through aesthetic contemplation, compassion, and ultimately the denial of the will to live. The work profoundly influenced Nietzsche, Wagner, Wittgenstein, and Freud.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the ultimate nature of reality, and what does it mean for human existence?
argumentCard.thesis: The world is, on one side, my representation (structured by the mind's forms) and, on the other, Will — a blind, purposeless striving that is the inner nature of everything and the root of all suffering.
keyMoves: Extends Kant: the thing-in-itself is knowable through inner experience as Will, not just unknowable noumenon
Argues that individuation is an illusion (principium individuationis) and that all beings share one Will
Offers three paths to liberation from suffering: aesthetic contemplation (temporary), compassion (ethical), and ascetic denial of the will (permanent)
argumentCard.strongestObjection: If the Will is truly blind and purposeless, it is unclear how it can be "denied" by an act of will, since the very capacity for denial would itself be an expression of Will.

title: Confessions
author: Saint Augustine
introduction: Written around 397 CE, the Confessions is both an autobiography of Augustine's conversion to Christianity and a philosophical meditation on memory, time, and the nature of evil. Augustine traces his journey through Manichaeism and Neoplatonism to Christian faith, arguing that the restless human heart finds peace only in God. The work invented the genre of spiritual autobiography and shaped Western ideas about interiority and selfhood.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the nature of the self, and where does it find rest?
argumentCard.thesis: The human soul is restless until it rests in God; evil is not a substance but a privation of good, and the journey inward through memory leads ultimately to the divine ground of being.
keyMoves: Narrates conversion as philosophical argument: each failed attempt at happiness (rhetoric, Manichaeism, sensuality) reveals a truth about human nature
Develops the privation theory of evil: evil has no positive existence but is the absence or corruption of good
Analyzes time as a distension of the soul — past (memory), present (attention), future (expectation) — challenging the reality of objective time
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Augustine's account of evil as mere privation struggles with the sheer force and specificity of cruelty and suffering: calling genocide a "lack of good" seems to trivialize its positive horror.

title: Laws
author: Plato
introduction: Composed near the end of Plato's life around 347 BCE, the Laws is his longest dialogue and most practical work of political philosophy. Unlike the Republic's ideal city ruled by philosopher-kings, the Laws designs a realistic second-best state governed by detailed written laws. An Athenian Stranger leads the conversation, discussing legislation on education, religion, crime, property, and family in a projected colony on Crete.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What laws and institutions would produce the best achievable state?
argumentCard.thesis: Since philosopher-kings are unavailable, the best practicable state is one ruled by wise, detailed laws that shape citizens' character through education, religion, and carefully designed institutions.
keyMoves: Abandons the philosopher-king model for a rule of law that constrains rulers as much as citizens
Grounds legislation in a theology that proves the existence and providence of the gods
Designs institutions (nocturnal council, graduated property classes, compulsory education) to promote virtue in the entire citizenry
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The Laws' pervasive regulation of private life — including music, drinking parties, and religious belief — creates a paternalistic state that modern readers may find oppressively controlling.

title: Phaedo
author: Plato
introduction: Set on the day of Socrates' execution in 399 BCE, the Phaedo presents Socrates' arguments for the immortality of the soul and his serene acceptance of death. Socrates argues that the soul is akin to the eternal, unchanging Forms and that philosophy is a preparation for death — the separation of soul from body. The dialogue is both a moving portrait of Socrates' last hours and a foundational text of Western metaphysics.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Is the soul immortal, and should the philosopher fear death?
argumentCard.thesis: The soul is immortal because it participates in the Form of Life and cannot admit its opposite (death); therefore the philosopher, who has spent life purifying the soul from bodily attachments, should welcome death.
keyMoves: The argument from recollection: learning is remembering what the soul knew before embodiment, proving pre-existence
The affinity argument: the soul resembles the invisible, unchanging Forms rather than the visible, changing body
The final argument: the soul, whose essential nature is to bring life, cannot admit death any more than fire can admit cold
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The arguments prove at most that the soul is different from the body, not that it survives bodily death: something can be non-physical and still cease to exist.

title: Phaedrus
author: Plato
introduction: Composed around 370 BCE, the Phaedrus is a dialogue on love, beauty, rhetoric, and the soul set outside the walls of Athens — Plato's only dialogue with an outdoor setting. Socrates delivers a mythic account of the soul as a charioteer driving two horses (reason, spirit, appetite) and argues that true rhetoric must be grounded in knowledge of the soul. The dialogue also contains Plato's famous critique of writing.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the nature of love, and what makes rhetoric genuinely persuasive?
argumentCard.thesis: Love is a divine madness that, properly directed, leads the soul upward toward the Forms; true rhetoric, unlike mere flattery, requires knowledge of the soul's nature and the truth about its subject.
keyMoves: Presents the myth of the charioteer: the soul has three parts, and love's frenzy can elevate or degrade depending on which part dominates
Argues that beauty perceived through the senses is the most accessible reminder of the transcendent Forms, sparking philosophical eros
Critiques writing as inferior to living speech because it cannot respond to questions or choose its audience
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Plato's critique of writing is delivered in writing, and his own dialogues are among the most influential texts in history — suggesting that the medium is more capable than his argument allows.

title: Philebus
author: Plato
introduction: Written around 355 BCE, the Philebus examines whether the good life consists primarily in pleasure or in wisdom. Socrates argues that neither pure pleasure nor pure intellect is sufficient: the best human life is a measured mixture of both, with reason providing the order and limit that make pleasure genuinely good. The dialogue is Plato's most sustained treatment of the nature of the good.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Is pleasure or wisdom the highest good in human life?
argumentCard.thesis: Neither pleasure alone nor wisdom alone is the good; the best life is a rightly proportioned mixture of both, in which reason supplies measure, beauty, and truth.
keyMoves: Classifies all things into four kinds: the unlimited (pleasure), the limit (measure/reason), the mixture, and the cause of mixture
Analyzes pleasures into true and false, pure and mixed, showing that many apparent pleasures contain hidden pain
Ranks the components of the good life: measure, beauty, intellect, the sciences, then pure pleasures — placing pleasure last
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The dialogue never clearly explains what makes a mixture "right" apart from asserting that reason must dominate, which begs the question against hedonism rather than refuting it.

title: Statesman
author: Plato
introduction: Written around 360 BCE as a companion to the Sophist, the Statesman uses the method of division to define the art of statesmanship. The Eleatic Stranger argues that the true statesman is a weaver who harmonizes opposing temperaments in the city and that rule by a wise individual is superior to rule of law, though law is the best available substitute. The dialogue marks an important development in Plato's political thought.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the art of the statesman, and how does it relate to law?
argumentCard.thesis: The statesman's art is a royal weaving that blends courageous and temperate natures into a harmonious civic fabric; ideal rule is by expert knowledge, but written law is the necessary second-best.
keyMoves: Uses extended division (diairesis) to distinguish the statesman from related figures (herdsman, merchant, priest)
Introduces the "Allegory of the Reversed Cosmos" to illustrate how political order requires constant intelligent maintenance
Argues that rigid laws cannot match the flexibility of genuine political wisdom but are preferable to unwise discretion
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The claim that one wise ruler is better than law opens the door to tyranny, since there is no reliable way to identify such a person or prevent their power from corrupting them.

title: Timaeus
author: Plato
introduction: Composed around 360 BCE, the Timaeus presents Plato's cosmology through the character Timaeus of Locri, who describes how a divine craftsman (Demiurge) fashioned the visible cosmos as a living image of the eternal Forms. The dialogue covers the creation of the world-soul, the elements, time, and the human body. It was the most widely read Platonic text in the medieval West and profoundly shaped Christian and Islamic cosmological thought.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: How and why was the physical universe created?
argumentCard.thesis: The cosmos is the work of a benevolent Demiurge who, looking to the eternal Forms as a model, imposed mathematical order on pre-existing chaotic matter, creating the best possible physical world.
keyMoves: Introduces the Demiurge (divine craftsman) as a cause distinct from both Forms and matter
Constructs the elements from geometric solids (fire=tetrahedron, earth=cube, etc.), grounding physics in mathematics
Describes the creation of the world-soul from a mixture of Being, Sameness, and Difference, making the cosmos a rational, living organism
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The account is presented as a "likely story" (eikos mythos), raising the question of whether Plato intended it as genuine cosmology or as an illustrative myth not to be taken literally.

title: Ethics
author: Baruch Spinoza
introduction: Published posthumously in 1677, the Ethics is a deductive metaphysical system presented in geometric order (definitions, axioms, propositions, proofs). Spinoza argues that God and Nature are one substance with infinite attributes, that mind and body are two aspects of the same reality, and that human freedom consists in understanding the necessity of all things. The work was condemned as atheism in its own time but is now recognized as one of the supreme achievements of Western philosophy.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the nature of God, and how can human beings achieve freedom and blessedness?
argumentCard.thesis: There is only one substance (God or Nature), of which all things are modes; freedom and blessedness come not from free will (which is an illusion) but from understanding the necessary order of nature through reason.
keyMoves: Proves that only one substance can exist and identifies it with God/Nature (Deus sive Natura)
Argues that mind and body are not two substances but two attributes of the same substance, dissolving the Cartesian mind-body problem
Derives an ethics of liberation: by understanding our emotions as necessary natural phenomena, we transform passive affects into active ones and achieve intellectual love of God
argumentCard.strongestObjection: If everything follows necessarily from God's nature, then moral praise and blame seem meaningless, and Spinoza's own distinction between bondage and freedom becomes difficult to sustain.

title: Time and Free Will
author: Henri Bergson
introduction: Published in 1889 as Bergson's doctoral thesis, Time and Free Will argues that the problem of free will arises from a confusion between lived duration (duree) and spatialized, clock time. Bergson contends that when we experience time as it truly is — a continuous, qualitative flow rather than a series of measurable instants — the determinism-freedom dilemma dissolves. The work launched Bergson's influential critique of mechanistic science.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Is free will compatible with determinism?
argumentCard.thesis: The free-will problem is a pseudo-problem created by spatializing time; when we recover the experience of pure duration — qualitative, indivisible, flowing — determinism loses its grip and freedom becomes self-evident.
keyMoves: Distinguishes qualitative multiplicity (duration) from quantitative multiplicity (space), showing that consciousness belongs to the former
Argues that determinism depends on treating mental states as discrete, measurable units arranged in space — a distortion of lived experience
Shows that the "prediction" of a free act is impossible because the act is inseparable from the whole personality at the moment of decision
argumentCard.strongestObjection: If duration is truly indivisible and non-spatial, it becomes difficult to explain how science, which depends on measurement and spatial representation, can be so spectacularly successful in predicting natural phenomena.

title: Second Treatise of Government
author: John Locke
introduction: Published in 1689, the Second Treatise of Government argues that political authority derives from the consent of the governed and exists to protect natural rights to life, liberty, and property. Locke contends that in the state of nature individuals possess these rights independently of government, and that a government which violates them forfeits its legitimacy. The work profoundly influenced the American and French revolutions.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the legitimate basis of political authority?
argumentCard.thesis: Government is legitimate only when it rests on the consent of the governed and serves to protect the natural rights (life, liberty, property) that individuals possess in the state of nature.
keyMoves: Derives property rights from labor: by mixing one's labor with natural resources, one acquires a right to the product
Distinguishes the state of nature (governed by natural law and reason) from the state of war (governed by force)
Establishes the right of revolution: when government systematically violates natural rights, the people may dissolve it and reconstitute political authority
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The labor theory of property and the social contract both rely on a hypothetical state of nature that never existed; consent given by distant ancestors cannot bind present generations who never agreed to the arrangement.

title: On the Nature of Things
author: Lucretius
introduction: Written around 55 BCE, De Rerum Natura is a philosophical poem expounding the atomist physics and ethics of Epicurus. Lucretius argues that the universe consists entirely of atoms and void, that the soul is mortal, and that the gods do not intervene in human affairs. By removing the fear of death and divine punishment, the poem aims to liberate readers for a life of tranquil pleasure.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the nature of reality, and how should this knowledge affect how we live?
argumentCard.thesis: Everything is composed of eternal, indivisible atoms moving through void; the soul is mortal, the gods are indifferent, and understanding this frees us from the fears that cause human misery.
keyMoves: Explains all natural phenomena (weather, plague, perception, reproduction) through atomic motion and combination without divine intervention
Introduces the "swerve" (clinamen) — a slight random deviation in atomic motion — to account for free will and novelty
Argues that death is nothing to us: where death is, we are not; where we are, death is not
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The clinamen appears to be an ad hoc addition that contradicts the deterministic mechanics of the rest of the system: if atoms swerve randomly, this produces chance rather than the genuine free will Lucretius seeks.

title: Essays
author: Michel de Montaigne
introduction: First published in 1580 and continuously revised until Montaigne's death in 1592, the Essays invented the literary form that bears their name. Montaigne turns his own experience, reading, and self-observation into philosophical investigations of custom, education, death, friendship, and the limits of human knowledge. His motto "Que sais-je?" (What do I know?) expresses a skepticism that is humane rather than paralyzing.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What can we know about ourselves and the world through honest self-examination?
argumentCard.thesis: Self-knowledge reveals the inconstancy, vanity, and limitation of human nature; wisdom consists not in dogmatic certainty but in the humble, curious, and tolerant acceptance of our condition.
keyMoves: Uses personal experience and anecdote as philosophical evidence, making the self the primary object of inquiry
Deploys Pyrrhonian skepticism to undermine intellectual arrogance while preserving practical engagement with life
Argues that custom and habit, not reason, govern most human behavior, and that recognizing this should produce tolerance toward others
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Montaigne's skepticism, taken seriously, threatens to undermine the very moral and practical judgments he continues to make throughout the Essays — if we know nothing, how can we live wisely?

title: The Dawn of Day
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
introduction: Published in 1881, The Dawn of Day (Morgenrothe) is a collection of aphorisms that begins Nietzsche's sustained critique of morality. He examines how moral feelings arise from custom, fear, and vanity rather than from reason or divine command, and argues that freeing oneself from the tyranny of custom is the first step toward genuine self-knowledge. The work is gentler in tone than later writings but no less radical in its implications.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: What are the true origins of moral feelings and judgments?
argumentCard.thesis: Moral sentiments are not rational insights or divine commands but products of custom, habit, fear, and the drive for social approval; recognizing this opens the possibility of a freer, more honest life.
keyMoves: Traces moral feelings to pre-moral origins in custom, obedience, and the desire to avoid punishment
Analyzes pity, self-sacrifice, and conscience as disguised forms of self-interest and power
Envisions a "dawn" of new values created by individuals who have emancipated themselves from inherited morality
argumentCard.strongestObjection: If all morality is reducible to custom and self-interest, Nietzsche has no basis for preferring his own "free" values over conventional ones — the critique dissolves its own ground.

title: The Age of Reason
author: Thomas Paine
introduction: Published in 1794 during the French Revolution, The Age of Reason is a vigorous defense of deism and critique of organized religion. Paine argues that the natural world is the only genuine revelation of God and that the Bible, far from being divinely inspired, is a collection of human writings full of contradictions and cruelties. The work made Paine a hero to freethinkers and a villain to the orthodox on both sides of the Atlantic.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Is revealed religion credible, or should we trust reason and nature alone?
argumentCard.thesis: The only authentic revelation is the natural world itself; the Bible and organized churches are human fabrications that corrupt the simple truth of one God knowable through reason.
keyMoves: Distinguishes between belief in God (deism, which Paine affirms) and belief in churches and scriptures (which he rejects)
Subjects the Old and New Testaments to critical analysis, exposing contradictions, borrowed myths, and moral atrocities
Argues that science and observation of nature provide a surer path to the Creator than any scripture
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Paine's deism assumes that nature unambiguously reveals a benevolent Creator, but the problem of natural evil (disease, predation, disaster) suggests that nature's "revelation" is far more ambiguous than he allows.

title: Ion
author: Plato
introduction: One of Plato's shortest dialogues, composed around 380 BCE, the Ion examines whether the rhapsode Ion possesses a genuine art or is merely inspired. Socrates argues that Ion's ability to perform Homer brilliantly but no other poet shows that he operates not by knowledge but by divine possession, like a link in a magnetic chain running from the Muse through the poet to the performer. The dialogue raises foundational questions about the nature of artistic inspiration and expertise.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Does the poet or performer possess genuine knowledge, or is artistic excellence a form of divine inspiration?
argumentCard.thesis: Poetic and performative excellence is not a rational art (techne) but divine inspiration (theia moira): the Muse possesses the poet, who possesses the performer, who moves the audience — a chain of magnetic enthusiasm.
keyMoves: Shows that Ion can speak brilliantly about Homer but not about other poets, proving his skill is not a general expertise
Introduces the magnet analogy: inspiration flows from the Muse through a chain of intermediaries, none of whom possess knowledge
Forces a dilemma: either Ion speaks by art (but then he should know all poets equally) or by inspiration (but then he has no real knowledge)
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The dilemma is false: domain-specific expertise is common (a Shakespeare scholar need not be equally expert on Dante), so Ion's specialization does not prove he lacks knowledge.

title: First Principles
author: Herbert Spencer
introduction: Published in 1862, First Principles is the foundational volume of Spencer's Synthetic Philosophy, which attempted to unify all knowledge under the law of evolution. Spencer argues that reality consists of an unknowable ultimate force manifesting through the universal process of evolution — the redistribution of matter and motion from homogeneity to heterogeneity. The work was enormously influential in the late nineteenth century.

argumentCard.centralQuestion: Is there a single law that governs all phenomena — physical, biological, psychological, and social?
argumentCard.thesis: All phenomena are instances of one universal process: evolution, defined as the integration of matter and dissipation of motion, producing ever greater heterogeneity, definiteness, and coherence.
keyMoves: Divides reality into the Knowable (phenomena, governed by scientific law) and the Unknowable (ultimate reality, beyond human comprehension)
Formulates the law of evolution as applying to nebulae, organisms, minds, and societies alike
Argues that dissolution (the reverse of evolution) is equally universal, so all complex structures eventually break down
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The "law of evolution" is so abstract that it explains everything and therefore nothing: calling stellar formation, biological adaptation, and social change all "evolution" papers over the radically different mechanisms at work in each domain.
